blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَنزِيلًا (٢٣)
Indeed We have sent down to you the Quran in a gradual descent. (23)
فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ ءَاثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا (٢٤)
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord and do not obey any sinner or ingrate from among them, (24)
وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٢٥)
and celebrate the Name of your Lord morning and evening, (25)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَٱسْجُدْ لَهُۥ وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا (٢٦)
and worship Him for a watch of the night and glorify Him the night long. (26)
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ وَيَذَرُونَ وَرَآءَهُمْ يَوْمًا ثَقِيلًا (٢٧)
Indeed they love this transitory life, and disregard a heavy day that is ahead of them. (27)
نَّحْنُ خَلَقْنَـٰهُمْ وَشَدَدْنَآ أَسْرَهُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا شِئْنَا بَدَّلْنَآ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٨)
We created them and strengthened their joints, and We will replace them with others like them whenever We like. (28)
إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (٢٩)
This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way toward his Lord. (29)
وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٣٠)
But you will not wish unless it is willed by Allah. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (30)
يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَآءُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًۢا (٣١)
He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and He has prepared a painful punishment for the wrongdoers. (31)

(Commentary)

When He described the reward of the righteous (الأبرار) and the everlasting bliss and magnificent kingdom He has ordained for them due to their patience for the sake of Allah, He directed the discourse toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He commanded him to be patient for the decree of his Lord and not to obey those sinners and disbelievers who love the immediate world (العاجلة), are attached to it, and turn away from the Hereafter—including the polytheists, the rest of the disbelievers, the hypocrites, and the people of base desires. He further commanded him to remember the Name of his Lord, prostrate to Him, and glorify Him continuously. Then, He generalized the ruling to his community by His saying: إِنَّ هَذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا "This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take a way to his Lord." (76:29)

This is the aspect of the verses' connection to what preceded them. Despite this, their context (سياق) is not devoid of resemblance to Meccan contexts. Even if they are considered Meccan, the beginning of the Surah would be Medinan and its conclusion Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ تَنْزِيلًا "Indeed We have sent down the Quran to you in a gradual descent." (76:23) The opening of the speech with إِنَّ (Indeed), the repetition of the first-person plural pronoun (نحن), and the use of the absolute object (المفعول المطلق) — the cognate accusative used for emphasis — are all for the purpose of corroboration (التأكيد). It is to establish that what was revealed of the Quran in separate stages (نجوماً متفرقة) is from Allah, the Glorified; no satanic prompting has entered it, nor is it from the self (نفساني).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا "So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, and do not obey any sinner or ingrate among them." (76:24) This is a derivation (تفريع) from what is necessitated by the content of the previous verse. For the necessity of Allah, the Glorified, being the One who revealed the Quran to him is that whatever decree (حكم) is in the Quran is the decree of his Lord which must be obeyed. Thus, the meaning is: since its revelation is from Us, then the decree it contains is the decree of your Lord, and it is obligatory upon you to be patient for it; so be patient for the decree of your Lord.

His saying, وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا "and do not obey any sinner or ingrate among them": The occurrence of the disjunctive "or" (أو) in the context of a prohibition (النهي) implies the generality of the ruling (عموم الحكم). Thus, the prohibition applies to obeying either of them, whether they are together or separate. It appears that the "sinner" (آثم) refers to one who is embroiled in disobedience (المعصية), and the "ingrate" (كفور) refers to one who is excessive in disbelief (الكفر). Thus, the verse encompasses all disbelievers and transgressors (الفساق).

The fact that the prohibition of obeying the sinner and the ingrate is preceded by the command to be patient for the decree of his Lord implies that the prohibition explains the command. The sense of the prohibition is: do not obey a sinner among them if he calls you to his sin, nor an ingrate if he calls you to his disbelief, because the sin of the sinner and the disbelief of the disbeliever are contrary to the decree of your Lord. As for linking the ruling to a description that suggests causality (العلية), it only implies that sin and disbelief are the causes for the prohibition of obedience absolutely, not just when the sinner calls to his specific sin or the disbeliever to his specific disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا "And celebrate the Name of your Lord morning and evening," (76:25) meaning: persist in the remembrance of your Lord—which is prayer (الصلاة)—in every morning (بكرة) and evening (أصيل), which are the early day (الغدو) and the late day (العشي).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَاسْجُدْ لَهُ وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا "and worship Him for a part of the night, and glorify Him through the long night." (76:26) The word "from" (من) denotes partitivity (التبعيض), and the "prostration" (السجود) intended here is prayer. The mention in these two verses of remembering His Name morning and evening, and prostrating to Him for part of the night, is susceptible to being applied to the Dawn (الصبح), Afternoon (العصر), Maghrib, and Isha prayers. This supports the view that these verses were revealed in Mecca before the five obligatory prayers were mandated by His saying in the verse of al-Isra: أَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلَى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ "Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital." (17:78)

Thus, these two verses are like His saying, the Exalted: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ "Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night." (11:114), and His saying: وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِها وَمِنْ آناءِ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرافَ النَّهارِ "and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting, and glorify Him in the watches of the night and at the day’s ends." (20:130)

Indeed, it has been said that since "evening" (الأصيل) can be applied to the time after the sun's decline (الزوال), His saying وَأَصِيلًا would include the times of both the Noon (الظهر) and Afternoon (العصر) prayers; this is not without merit.

His saying, وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا "and glorify Him through the long night": meaning, during a long night. Describing the night as "long" is explanatory (توضيحي), not restrictive (احترازي). The "glorification" (التسبيح) intended is the Night Prayer (صلاة الليل). It is also possible that "long" (طويلاً) is an adjective for a deleted absolute object, the estimation being: "glorify Him in the night with a long glorification (تسبيحاً طويلاً)."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هَؤُلَاءِ يُحِبُّونَ الْعَاجِلَةَ وَيَذَرُونَ وَرَاءَهُمْ يَوْمًا ثَقِيلًا "Indeed they love the transitory life, and leave behind them a heavy day." (76:27) This is a justification (تعليل) for the preceding command and prohibition. The demonstration "these" (هؤلاء) refers to the collective of the sinner and the ingrate, as indicated by the use of indefinite nouns in the context of prohibition. The "transitory life" (العاجلة) refers to the life of this world. Describing the day as "heavy" (ثقيلاً) is a metaphor (استعارة); its heaviness refers to its severity (شدته), as if it were a heavy burden difficult to carry. The "day" is the Day of Resurrection.

The fact that the day is "behind them" (وراءهم) can mean it is in front of them, because "behind" (وراء) can convey the meaning of encompassing (الإحاطة). Or it means they have cast it behind them and behind their backs, based on the word "leave" (يذرون) conveying the sense of turning away (الإعراض).

The meaning is: be patient for the decree of your Lord and maintain the prayer, and do not obey the sinners and disbelievers among them, because these sinners and disbelievers love the life of this world and do not act except for it, and they leave in front of them a severe day—or they turn away and cast behind them a severe day—which they shall surely meet.

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ خَلَقْنَاهُمْ وَشَدَدْنَا أَسْرَهُمْ وَإِذَا شِئْنَا بَدَّلْنَا أَمْثَالَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا "We created them and strengthened their joints, and when We wish We will replace them with others like them." (76:28) "Strengthening" (الشد) is the opposite of loosening. "Joints/Binding" (الأسر) originally means tying and binding, and it is applied to that which binds. Thus, the meaning of "We strengthened their joints" is: We made firm the binding of their joints with ligaments, nerves, and muscles. Or "binding" (الأسر) means the "bound one" (المأسور), and the meaning is: We made firm the connection of their various parts, bound one to another, until each of them became a single human being through that.

His saying, وَإِذَا شِئْنَا بَدَّلْنَا أَمْثَالَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا "and when We wish We will replace them with others like them": meaning, if We willed, We would replace them with their likes; We would take them away and bring others like them in their place, which refers to the death of one generation and the life of another. It has been said that it refers to changing their worldly form into the form of the Resurrection, but this is far-fetched given the context.

The verse serves to repel an objection (دفع الدخل) that might be imagined—that by their love for the world and their turning away from the Hereafter, they frustrate Him, the Exalted, and spoil His will for them to believe and obey. Thus, the response is that they are created by Allah; He created them and strengthened their frame, and if He wills, He removes them and brings others. So how can they frustrate Him when their creation, their command, their life, and their death are in His hand?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هَذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا "This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take a way to his Lord." (76:29) The explanation of this has preceded in Surah al-Muzzammil. The demonstration "this" (هذه) refers to what has been mentioned in the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا "But you do not wish unless Allah wishes. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." (76:30) The exception from the negation implies that the servant's will (مشية العبد) is dependent in its existence upon His will, the Exalted. Thus, the will of Allah, the Exalted, has an effect on the servant's act by way of its attachment to the servant's will. It is not attached to the servant's act independently and without intermediary, such that it would necessitate the nullification of the effect of the servant's volition and the act being compelled (جبرياً). Nor is the servant independent in his will, doing whatever he wishes whether Allah wishes or not. Thus, the act is volitional (اختياري) because it is attributed to the servant's choice (اختيار). As for the servant's choice itself, it is not attributed to another choice. The clarification of this discussion has been repeated in several preceding places.

The verse is intended to repel the illusion that they are independent in their will, disconnected from the will of their Lord. Perhaps the establishment of this reminder for them is the reason for the shift (الالتفات) to direct address in His saying, وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ "But you do not wish unless Allah wishes." Similarly, the reason for the shift from the first-person plural to the third person in His saying, يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ "Allah wishes. Indeed Allah..." is to point to the cause of the ruling. For the One named by this Majestic Name (الله) is the One from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything ends; thus, no will exists except by His will, and no will is effective except by His permission.

His saying, إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا "Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise," is a preamble to explaining the content of the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا "He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and for the wrongdoers He has prepared a painful punishment." (76:31) The object of "He wishes" (يشاء) is omitted, as indicated by the speech; the estimation is: "He admits into His mercy whomever He wishes to admit into His mercy." He does not wish except the admission of those who believe and are God-conscious. As for others—the people of sin and disbelief—He explained their state by His saying: وَالظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا "and for the wrongdoers He has prepared a painful punishment."

The verse explains His established sunnah (سنته) — the divine law and habitual way of acting — regarding His servants in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء). This has been justified by what was at the end of the previous verse: إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا "Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." Thus, He conveyed that the sunnah of Allah, the Exalted, is not a haphazard sunnah based on ignorance; rather, He treats each of the two groups with what they deserve, and He will soon inform them of the reality of what they used to do.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that Abd ibn Humayd, ibn Jarir, and ibn Abi Hatim reported from Qatadah regarding His saying: وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا "and do not obey any sinner or ingrate among them." He said: "It was narrated to us that it was revealed concerning the enemy of Allah, Abu Jahl."

I say: This is more akin to an application (التطبيق) — the mapping of a general verse onto a specific historical instance.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا "and glorify Him through the long night." It is narrated from Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) that Ahmad ibn Muhammad asked him about this verse and said: "What is that glorification?" He replied: "The Night Prayer (صلاة الليل)."

In al-Khara'ij wa al-Jara'ih, narrated from the Qāʾim (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition where he says to Kamil ibn Ibrahim al-Madani: "And you came to ask about the claim of the Mufawwidah (المفوضة) — those who believe Allah delegated all affairs to the creation and no longer intervenes. They have lied! Rather, our hearts are vessels for the will of Allah (عز وجل); so when He wishes, we wish, and Allah says: وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ 'But you do not wish unless Allah wishes.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ibn Mardawayh narrated via ibn Shihab from Salim from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to say when he delivered a sermon: كل ما هو آت قريب ، لا بعد لما يأتي ، ولا يعجل الله لعجلة أحد ، ما شاء الله لا ما شاء الناس ، يريد الناس أمرا ويريد الله أمرا ، ما شاء الله كان ولو كره الناس ، لا مباعد لما قرب الله ، ولا مقرب لما باعد الله ، لا يكون شيء إلا بإذن الله. "Everything that is coming is near; there is no 'far' for what is to come. Allah does not hasten for the haste of anyone. What Allah wishes [happens], not what people wish. People intend a matter and Allah intends a matter; what Allah wishes comes to be, even if people dislike it. There is none to distance what Allah has brought near, and none to bring near what Allah has distanced. Nothing occurs except by the permission of Allah."

I say: In some narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), there is an application of the ruling in His saying فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ "So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord" and the "mercy" in His saying يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ "He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy" to the Wilayah (الولاية) — the divinely appointed authority and guardianship. This is of the nature of al-jary (الجري) — the ongoing applicability of the Quran to new instances — or the inner meaning (البطن), and is not part of the formal exegesis (التفسير) at all.
